images.caption: Figure 2:Incremental representation of different window sizes, ranging from 7 tokens per window up to 60 (the complete 120 representation is not included).

Figure 3:Comparison of performance betweenSourceTrackerand the Winnowing method, conducted to determine the optimal number of snippets to retrieve in the initial stage ofHST, aiming to match Winnowing’s effectiveness. The plot shows retrieval performance at R@100 forSourceTrackerand R@1 for Winnowing, averaged across dataset sizes from10001000to100 000100\,000samples. Solid lines depict exact match variants, while dashed lines indicate always-replacement variants. Our model (blue) consistently achieves retrieval performance that is equal to or exceeds Winnowing (red). This guarantees that the first-stage retrieval maintains the overall effectiveness of the Winnowing process.

Figure 4:The figures illustrate the Recall@1 (R@1) across window sizes ranging from 7 to 480 tokens with three different dimensions of search space—10001000,10 00010\,000, and100 000100\,000samples. We emphasize that both dataset size and window size significantly influence the performance ofSourceTrackerand the Winnowing algorithm. WhileSourceTrackerperforms better with larger windows (up to 240 tokens), the Winnowing algorithm shows greater resilience to variations in both window size and dataset size.

Figure 5:MRR (%) comparison across different window sizes betweenSourceTracker, Winnowing, andHST. Each subplot reports the performance trend as the dataset size increases, while different panels correspond to different window sizes. The proposedHSTconsistently matches or outperforms the baseline methods for window sizes larger than 30 tokens, while preserving the same logarithmic-time retrieval complexity asSourceTracker, enabled by Qdrant-based indexing.

Figure 6:Qdrant query latency performance across different collection sizes and top-k values. The plots demonstrate consistent logarithmic scaling behavior with minimal latency increase even at large scales. Extrapolated performance (cross markers) shows the system maintaining sub-second response times even at billions of samples, confirming the theoretical O(log N) complexity advantage over traditional fingerprinting methods.

Figure 7:Single‑query latency of theMOSSsystem (Winnowing) versus dataset size. A fitted power‑law modelT​(N)=a​NbT(N)=aN^{b}(a=1.294×10−9a=1.294\times 10^{-9},b=1.0573b=1.0573) indicates near‑linear scaling (b≈1b\approx 1). Both axes are logarithmic; The fitted model is used to extrapolate latency atN=109N=10^{9},101010^{10},101110^{11}samples (cross markers).

Figure 8:Results of the LLM error similarity evaluation: The left figure illustrates how different clone type errors (verbatim clone type 1 and frequent word replacement clone type 2) result in distinct suggestions, leading to worse recommendations from our system when fragments are adapted. Spearman rank correlations revealed significant monotonic relationships between context window size and similarity scores (Clone Type 1: rs = 0.446, p<<0.001; Clone Type 2: rs = 0.360, p<<0.001). The second figure shows the similarity error results aggregated by clone type. Both figures indicate that larger context windows in generation lead to more inherent snippets retrieved.
